title: The Eighteenth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver
## September 22, 2024

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Eighteenth Sunday after Pentecost falls in Ordinary Time, specifically in the long green season following Pentecost and before Advent. Under the Revised Common Lectionary (RCL), the readings for this Sunday would likely be Proverbs 31:10-31 (or Amos 8:4-7), Psalm 113, James 3:13-4:3 (or 3:13-4:8), and Mark 9:30-37. The emphasis in the Gospel account of Jesus teaching about servanthood and welcoming children would have been central to Richard Lawson's sermon. This is a period of sustained focus on Christian living and discipleship outside the major liturgical seasons.

EPISCOPAL/ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a relatively quiet period between General Convention 2024 (held in Louisville in June) and the fall diocesan activities. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Kimberly Chastain (elected in 2023), was settling into regular programming and fall stewardship campaigns across the diocese. No major denominational crises or notable bishop elections were pending at this specific moment, though ongoing discussions about LGBTQ+ inclusion and Anglican Communion unity continued in background ecclesiastical conversations.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing early fall weather with temperatures in the 70s-80s Fahrenheit, typical for late September. The Colorado legislature was not in session; attention in Colorado Springs was focused on ongoing water rights disputes and air quality concerns common to the season. Downtown Denver, where Saint John's Cathedral is located in the Capitol Hill neighborhood, was preparing for the fall tourism season and experiencing typical mid-September economic activity without major acute crises.

NATIONAL NEWS

The 2024 U.S. presidential campaign was in full intensity, with roughly six weeks until the November election; Vice President Kamala Harris and former President Donald Trump were engaged in the homestretch of campaigning. Hurricane Helene was making landfall along the U.S. Southeast coast around this exact date (September 26), which would have been developing in the news throughout the week before this sermon. The UAW auto workers' strike was ongoing, having begun September 15 with targeted actions at Ford, GM, and Stellantis facilities.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The war in Ukraine continued with active fighting, particularly in eastern regions, as Russia maintained pressure despite significant military losses through the year. The Middle East remained tense following months of Israel-Gaza conflict with no immediate resolution in sight. Global economic concerns about inflation and central bank interest rate policies dominated international financial news.

CULTURAL MOMENT

America was in late-summer-to-early-fall transition with college football season underway (the Colorado Buffaloes would have been in their football season). The country was deeply polarized heading into the final stretch of the presidential election, with anxiety and activism at elevated levels. There was a pervasive sense of national fatigue mixed with stakes-consciousness—people were aware of consequential elections and crises but also seeking stability and normalcy, making themes of Christian servanthood and faith particularly resonant in parish settings.
